key_messages: Dry and seasonably warm weather will continue through the end of the week as high pressure settles over the region.
afd.what_changed: As of 216 PM EDT Thursday... No significant changes to the forecast.
afd.raw:
000
FXUS61 KBTV 240011
AFDBTV

Area Forecast Discussion
National Weather Service Burlington VT
811 PM EDT Thu Jul 23 2026

.WHAT HAS CHANGED...
As of 216 PM EDT Thursday...

No significant changes to the forecast.

&&

.KEY MESSAGES...
As of 216 PM EDT Thursday...

1. Dry and seasonably warm weather will continue through the 
end of the week as high pressure settles over the region.

2. Unsettled weather returns next week.

&&

.DISCUSSION...
As of 216 PM EDT Thursday...

KEY MESSAGE 1: Surface high pressure is beginning to build into the 
region from the west, where is is currently centered over the Great 
Lakes. A few diurnally driven fair weather cumulus clouds have 
developed over the higher terrain and downslope regions of the 
Adirondacks, but otherwise the rest of today will remain clear and 
pleasant. Breezy conditions on the periphery of the high will weaken 
towards this evening with winds trending towards calm Our incoming 
airmass for the weekend will be quite dry with relative humidities 
falling to near 25-30% with dewpoints into the upper 40s to near 50 
tomorrow and Saturday. Given how low dewpoints will be with clear 
overnight skies, good radiational cooling is expected tonight and 
tomorrow night with temperatures cooling to the upper 40s to upper 
50s (warmer locations near the Champlain Valley due to the warmer 
lake temperatures). A few favored locations in the Adirondacks and 
eastern Vermont could even lower to the low to mid 40s tonight and 
Friday night. As the center of the high moves overhead Friday into 
Saturday, winds will remain calm, with light channeled flow in the 
Champlain Valley which could bring some light southerly breezes 
around 5-10 mph. Temperatures will gradually warm tomorrow and 
Saturday with highs increasing towards the upper 80s by Saturday. 
Thankfully, humidity will remain low with an unfavorable moisture 
advection pattern. A pattern change will begin late Sunday with the 
approach of a weak cold front as our pattern turns more troughy 
into next week. General expectations are that the bulk of any 
precipitation will hold off until Sunday night, though a few 
isolated terrain driven showers may be possible Sunday afternoon in 
the Adirondacks.

KEY MESSAGE 2: A weak omega block is slated to develop across the US 
for much of next week as the Bermuda high across the western 
Atlantic builds northward with a strengthening ridge also expected 
across the west/central US. This will leave New England underneath 
an upper level trough which will likely bring diurnal rain and/or 
thunderstorm chances with temperatures at or slightly below seasonal 
normals. Monday and Tuesday right now appear to be the wettest days 
of the upcoming week as a cold front should slowly push through the 
region as a lack of deep layer flow will struggle to push the front 
eastward. This could lead to a period of moderate rainfall but 
rainfall rates themselves will not be too impressive given the 
deeper moisture will be shunted much further south and east. Behind 
the cold front on Wednesday, we will see steep mid-level lapse rates 
due to cooler air sinking south which will support diurnal showers 
and maybe a rumble of thunder. WPC does have us in a marginal ERO 
for Monday but the lack of deep moisture will likely preclude any 
flooding potential.

&&

.AVIATION /00Z FRIDAY THROUGH TUESDAY/...
Through 00Z Saturday...VFR conditions overnight with just a
small (10-15%) probability of IFR or lower conditions near
sunrise at MPV/SLK in some shallow ground fog. However, given
dry air at the surface, still some boundary layer winds, and 2
days removed from rainfall, feel potential is low at this time.
Otherwise, any lingering mid level clouds will dissipate with
mostly clear skies overnight and light south-southwest winds 2
to 6 knots. VFR conditions continue on Friday with south to
southwest winds 5 to 10 knots as high pressure prevails. 

Outlook...

Friday Night: VFR. NO SIG WX.
Saturday: VFR. NO SIG WX.
Saturday Night: VFR. NO SIG WX.
Sunday: VFR. Slight chance SHRA, Slight chance TSRA.
Sunday Night: VFR. Slight chance SHRA.
Monday: Mainly VFR, with local IFR possible. Chance SHRA, Slight
chance TSRA.
Monday Night: VFR. Chance SHRA, Slight chance TSRA.
Tuesday: Mainly VFR, with local IFR possible. Likely SHRA, Chance
TSRA.

&&

.BTV WATCHES/WARNINGS/ADVISORIES...
VT...None.
NY...None.

&&

$$

WHAT HAS CHANGED...Danzig
DISCUSSION...Danzig/Clay
AVIATION...Taber
